data.title: Verizon Communications shares decline for the seventh consecutive session
data.description:
Verizon Communications (NYSE:VZ) shares fell 0.14% Tuesday to $45.36, extending the losing streak to seven sessions.

Over the preceding six sessions, the stock declined more than 8.06%, compared with a 4.61% gain in the S&P 500 Index. Despite the recent weakness, the shares are still up over 8.86% year to date, outperforming the index’s 0.60% rise.

Seeking Alpha analyst Bela Lakos maintained a Hold rating on VZ, citing attractive fundamentals supported by strong Q4 subscriber growth and a targeted $5B in annual cost savings.

However, the analyst flagged risks from rising debt levels and an uncertain interest rate environment, which could weigh on profitability. While the company’s free cash flow outlook and shareholder returns remain solid, the current valuation suggests limited upside, though income-focused investors may be drawn to its 5.6% dividend yield.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating also supports a Hold stance, with a score of 3.49 out of 5. The grading reflects a mixed profile for Verizon Communications, with an A+ for profitability indicating strong margins and earnings stability, a D for growth pointing to weak expansion, and a B+ for valuation proposing the stock is fairly priced with limited upside.

Separately, Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts have assigned Buy ratings to Verizon Communications. However, Quant scores of 3.76 and 3.54 indicate the ratings are approaching Hold territory, suggesting a more cautious stance on the stock.

data.title: S&P 500 ends more than 1% as Trump hints of 'imminent' talks with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages closed comfortably in the green on Tuesday as investors grew optimistic about potential U.S.-Iran peace talks that could ease tensions in the Middle East.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) finished +1.2%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) ended +0.7%.

The gains followed comments from President Donald Trump indicating that Iran had reached out for potential negotiations, even as the U.S. moved forward with a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump suggested that negotiations with Iran could take place “over the next two days” in Pakistan.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 4 basis points lower at 4.25%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 3 basis points to 3.75%.

“Crude oil prices are falling, stocks and bonds are higher, and markets have calmed even though the U.S. has blockaded the Strait of Hormuz. The markets are optimistic that the end of hostilities could be on the horizon,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Andrew Hecht.

“However, risks remain high in the current environment. We can hope the market is correct, but we must remain vigilant when approaching any risk positions, with careful attention to risk-reward dynamics,” Hecht added.

On the economic front, the March U.S. Producer Price Index came in at +0.5% MoM vs. +1.2% consensus and +0.5% prior (revised from +0.7%), and +4.0% YoY vs. +4.7% consensus and +3.4% prior.

Core PPI, which excludes foods and energy, was +0.1% MoM vs. +0.5% consensus and +0.3% prior (revised from +0.5%), and +3.8% YoY vs. +4.2% consensus and +3.8% prior (revised from +3.9%).

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were lower at $92.17, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $95.25 per barrel.

data.title: Caterpillar is said to acquire autonomous tractor startup Monarch
data.description:
Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) acquired agricultural technology startup Monarch Tractor, Bloomberg News reported Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter. The purchase marks a shift for a company that had struggled to scale its business.

Monarch disclosed in a recent LinkedIn post that its technology had been purchased by a large equipment manufacturer but did not name the buyer. Sources indicated that Caterpillar (CAT) was behind the deal, though neither company publicly confirmed the transaction.

The California-based startup, known for developing self-driving electric tractors, had faced operational challenges and cut jobs in recent months. In its statement, Monarch said it would pivot away from manufacturing complete vehicles and instead focus on licensing its technology for use in tractors and other heavy equipment.

The acquisition would align with Caterpillar’s (CAT) core business in construction and industrial machinery, an area where automation and electrification are becoming more prominent.

Monarch’s difficulties reflect broader pressures across the climate technology sector, where companies have struggled to turn early innovation into sustainable commercial operations. Several clean tech firms have recently downsized or sought restructuring as funding becomes more selective.

Investment trends also show waning interest in agricultural-focused climate startups. Data from BloombergNEF indicates that venture capital funding in the sector has declined sharply from earlier peaks, with totals falling significantly over the past two years and continuing to weaken in early 2026.

Before the reported deal, Monarch had raised about $251 million from investors including Astanor Ventures and funds affiliated with Foxconn. Founded in 2018 by a team that included a former Tesla Inc. executive, the company was part of a broader effort to develop lower-emission alternatives to traditional farm equipment.

Agriculture remains a meaningful source of greenhouse gas emissions, and a number of startups have sought to introduce electric machinery to reduce reliance on diesel-powered equipment. Some of those efforts have been consolidated in recent years, including acquisitions such as Solectrac by Ideanomics.

data.title: Norsk Hydro downgraded at Deutsche Bank after strong aluminum price rally
data.description:
Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) (NHYKF) down 3.8% in Tuesday's trading as Deutsche Bank downgraded the Norwegian aluminum and renewable energy company to Hold from Buy with a NOK100 price target, following this year's strong rally in the stock and aluminum prices.

If the flow of alumina into the Middle East does not improve soon, aluminum prices could spike even higher, and Norsk Hydro's (NHYDY) (NHYKF) Q2-Q3 EBITDA could approach the previous record of more than NOK11B in Q2 2022, but the longer the conflict lasts, the greater the demand risks, Deutsche Bank's Liam Fitzpatrick said.

The analyst estimated Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) (NHYKF) shares are currently discounting an aluminum price in the $3,200-$3,400/ton range, above incentive price levels and well above marginal cost.

Fitzpatrick also estimated current global outages from Iranian strikes and aluminum shortages are running at 3.5M-4M metric tons/year, or ~5% of global primary supply, putting the market in a sizeable near-term deficit; if the Strait of Hormuz does not open soon, further alumina shortage-led cuts are likely, and prices could spike to more than $4K/ton.

data.title: European coalition drafts postwar plan to free up the Strait of Hormuz without the U.S.
data.description:
European countries are drafting a plan to form a broad international coalition to secure the Strait of Hormuz after the current conflict ends—but without U.S. command, the Wall Street Journal reported.

French President Emmanuel Macron said Tuesday the mission would exclude “belligerent parties,” meaning the U.S., Israel, and Iran, with European diplomats confirming their ships would not operate under American command.

The plan has gained unexpected momentum with Germany’s likely participation, which could be announced as early as Thursday. Macron and U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer will host an online meeting Friday with several dozen countries to discuss policing the strait, though the U.S. will not attend.

French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot emphasized that “the mission we are referring to could only be deployed once calm has been restored and hostilities have ceased,” adding that the coalition would coordinate with Iran and Oman.

The operation involves three phases: establishing logistics to free hundreds of trapped ships, conducting a massive demining effort using Europe’s fleet of over 150 minesweeping vessels, and deploying frigates and destroyers for military escorts modeled on Operation Aspides in the Red Sea.

Europe’s minesweeping capabilities far exceed those of the U.S., which has largely decommissioned its fleet.

The plan reflects deepening trans-Atlantic tensions after U.S. President Donald Trump urged Europeans to join U.S. military efforts, including a blockade of Iranian ports. Macron dismissed reopening the strait by force as “unrealistic,” saying it would take “an infinite amount of time” and expose vessels to coastal threats.

European leaders have instead focused on freeing maritime traffic rather than restricting it further, rejecting Trump’s pressure amid widespread unpopularity of the conflict among European voters.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: GrafTech cut to Sell equivalent at JPM, pointing to limited liquidity runway to service debt load
data.description:
GrafTech International (EAF) down 0.7% in Tuesday's trading as J.P. Morgan downgraded the manufacturer of graphite electrodes and petroleum coke to Underweight from Neutral, viewing FY 2027 as a critical make-or-break year for the company amid a ~$100M/year free cash flow burn and a limited liquidity runway to service its debt load.

JPM analyst Bill Peterson said evolving U.S. trade policy could provide a much-needed impetus for non-sustainable pricing to finally inflect directionally higher, but this would depend largely on FY 2027 U.S. customer commitments, with negotiations not starting until later this year.

In the meantime, Peterson sees outsized risk to costs relative to steel mills, which are already benefitting from structurally higher steel pricing and displaced imports amid S232 tariffs, Peterson said, adding that if LDGE pricing does not inflect higher, then GrafTech (EAF) could face tough decisions such as curtailing further capacity or diluting shareholders to extend its liquidity runway; consolidation in the U.S. market could help, but prior precedent implies potential antitrust risks given only 2-3 domestic suppliers.

While commending GrafTech (EAF) management's efforts in eliminating fixed costs and navigating a structurally oversupplied market, Peterson said he prefers exposure to cash-generating steel mills until gaining further confidence in improving FY 2027 fundamentals, which could still be at least a few quarters away.

data.title: Mid-cap utilities stocks with A grade EPS revisions as earnings season begins
data.description:
As Q1 2026 earnings season approaches, the utilities sector is arriving at reporting season from a position of notable strength, the S&P 500 Utilities Index gained 8.3% in Q1 2026, significantly outperforming the broader market as investors rotated into defensive, income-generating names amid macroeconomic uncertainty.

This list below features mid-cap utilities stocks which contain Seeking Alpha EPS revision Quant Grades A.

The list contains only four stocks, led by American States Water Company (AWR), California Water Service Group (CWT) and Hawaiian Electric Industries (HE), all holding A grades. Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN), a gas utility, rounds out the list with an A- EPS revision grade.

  1. American States Water Company (AWR) – EPS Revision Grade: A

  2. California Water Service Group (CWT) – EPS Revision Grade: A

  3. Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. (HE) – EPS Revision Grade: A

  4. Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN) – EPS Revision Grade: A-

Utilities ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI)

data.title: Banks Q1 results offer views of private credit exposure
data.description:
With investors concerned about risk tied to private credit, some banks are disclosing their exposures to loans made to non-bank financial institutions in their Q1 earnings materials.

Citigroup (C) said its corporate private credit warehouse financing comprises $22B of loans, with 98% at investment grade. The bank said that portfolio has zero losses over its life. To provide a sense of scale, Citi had $762B of loans on its balance sheet as of March 31, 2026.

Citi also pointed out that such loans are to tier 1 asset managers, and loans were made to upper middle market companies (average EBITDA over $150M). In addition, less than 1% of loans are to business development companies ((BDCs)), which have been particularly hard hit by worries over private credit.

Wells Fargo (WFC) corporate debt finance to financials, excluding bank loans, stands at $36.2B of loans at March 31, 2026. About 23% of its exposure is to BDCs as the equity counterparty. By comparison, the bank had total loans of $996B at the end of the quarter.

By sector, Wells Fargo's (WFC) private credit exposure is biggest in business services at 19% of exposure based on collateral, followed by software at 17% and healthcare at 15%. "Other," at 18% of collateral, is made up of 12 industries with exposures of less than 3% each.

During Goldman Sachs's (GS) earnings call on Monday, Chairman and CEO David Solomon attributed most of the exits in private credit to retail investors. "When you look at our broad platform, it's over 80% institutional partners — very, very broad, very, very diverse," he said.

"Overall, we feel good about the long-term opportunity in private credit and our ability to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns for clients," he said.

Definitions are key in discussing the sector, Solomon said. In the broadest sense, private credit represents ~$3.5T of assets. "But the thing that's been getting a lot of focus is direct lending, and direct lending is about $1.6T to $1.7T of assets, of which the retail channel for that direct lending business is about 20%, or about $230B of NAV (net asset value)," he said.

Wells Fargo (WFC) stock dropped 5.1%, Citi (C) rose 3.4%, and Goldman Sachs (GS) increased 2.4% in late Tuesday trading.

data.title: JPMorgan forecasts 2026 NII ex Markets of about $95B while planning for a 5.2% G-SIB requirement in 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "This quarter, the firm reported net income of $16.5 billion and EPS of $5.94, with an ROTCE of 23%." (Executive VP & CFO Jeremy Barnum)

  • "Revenue of $50.5 billion was up 10% year-on-year" and "Expenses of $26.9 billion were up 14% year-on-year" while "credit costs" were "$2.5 billion with net charge-offs of $2.3 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "We ended the quarter with a standardized CET1 ratio of 14.3%, down 30 basis points versus the prior quarter" and "standardized RWA is up $60 billion, primarily driven by the Markets business." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "Under the proposed rules, our CET1 capital would increase around 4%" and "we need to plan for 5.2% in 2028"; Barnum also said the combined Basel III endgame and G-SIB effects imply "a total increase of about $20 billion of G-SIB capital based on our current balance sheet." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "Consumers and small businesses remain resilient with consumer spend growth continuing above last year's pace" and "in Home Lending, originations of $13.7 billion increased 46% year-on-year." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "IB fees were up 28% year-on-year" and Barnum cautioned that "developments in the Middle East could have an impact on deal execution and timing." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect NII ex Markets to be about $95 billion" and "we now expect total NII to be approximately $103 billion" as "market NII" is expected to decrease "to about $8 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "The adjusted expense outlook continues to be about $105 billion" and "the Card net charge-off rate continues to be approximately 3.4%." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • On why NII ex-Markets was unchanged despite rate shifts, Barnum said, "the amount of upward revision that you might have otherwise expected is really quite small" and "there were some other bits of up and down noise, and some rounding effects." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

Financial results

  • "CCB reported net income of $5 billion" on "Revenue of $19.6 billion"; Barnum also cited "Average deposits were up 2% year-on-year and quarter-on-quarter" and "Client investment assets were up 18% year-on-year." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "The CIB reported net income of $9 billion" on "revenue of $23.4 billion" and markets strength included "fixed income was up 21% year-on-year" and "Equities was up 17%." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • "AWM reported net income of $1.8 billion" with "Revenue of $6.4 billion"; Barnum highlighted "Long-term net inflows were $54 billion" and "AUM of $4.8 trillion" with "client assets of $7.1 trillion." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

Q&A

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: Asked about the AI cash tool and deposit competition; Chairman & CEO Jamie Dimon said, "we're trying to look at the world from the point of view of the customer" and Barnum added, "I think the right way to think of it is sort of as an experiment right now." (Chairman & CEO Dimon; Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • Chubak, Wolfe Research: Pressed on Basel III RWA mitigation and double counting; Barnum said, "I don't think this is a story about optimization at this point" and added that removal of double counting is supported but "probably not our #1 priority." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Asked why NII ex-Markets stayed at $95B; Barnum said the disclosed "EAR" was "$1.8 billion" and concluded, "I don't think there's too much to read into it." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • John McDonald, Truist: Asked about reserve setting; Barnum said, "we actually did not change the weights this quarter" and cited the "weighted average unemployment rate" moving "from 5.8% to 5.6%." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked about consumer cracks and energy prices; Barnum said, "There really is not anything new or interesting to say this quarter" and emphasized gas/energy was "something like 3%" of typical spend in their portfolio. (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Challenged expense run-rate vs. $105B guide; Barnum said, "I would somewhat discourage you from like annualizing quarterly expense run rate" while Dimon added, "The $105 billion is not a promise, it's an outcome of business results." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum; Chairman & CEO Dimon)

  • Cassidy, RBC: Asked about stablecoins and payments; Barnum said, "we're super excited to embrace this type of innovation" and highlighted "Kinexys" and "tokenized deposits," while warning against "regulatory arbitrage." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked about AI-driven cyber risk; Dimon said, "it is our largest risk" and "AI has made it worse, it's made it harder." (Chairman & CEO Dimon)

  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: Asked on capital allocation; Dimon said JPMorgan has "around $40 billion" of excess capital and that "Our preferred way of using capital is not buying back stock today." (Chairman & CEO Dimon)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical on deposits, capital rules, expense discipline, and systemic risks, reflected in questions about "deposit pressure," "RWA inflationary impacts," and annualizing expenses against the $105B outlook. (Multiple analysts)

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive on performance and business momentum (e.g., "net income of $16.5 billion" and resilient consumer), while turning defensive and critical on regulatory calibration, including Dimon calling the global market shock "completely out of whack in reality" and operational risk capital "another crazy obtuse" construct. (Executive VP & CFO Barnum; Chairman & CEO Dimon)

  • Versus the prior quarter, management discussion shifted from explaining Apple Card reserve dynamics and NBFI definitions to sharper focus on Basel III endgame/G-SIB reproposals and explicit planning for higher surcharges, alongside continued attention to stablecoins, AI, and private credit. (Executive VP & CFO Barnum; Chairman & CEO Dimon)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 results were framed around higher profitability and ROTCE (23% vs. 18% in Q4 2025), while Q4 2025 emphasized that results "included the previously announced reserve build of $2.2 billion" tied to Apple Card. (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • Full-year 2026 guideposts were reiterated quarter-to-quarter ("NII ex Markets" about $95B; total NII about $103B; adjusted expenses about $105B; card net charge-off rate about 3.4%), but Q1 2026 changed the composition by saying market NII is expected to decrease "to about $8 billion" (Q4 2025 described Markets NII "increasing to about $8 billion" due to rate cuts assumptions). (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4 2025 emphasis on stablecoin deposit risk and credit card APR caps to Q1 2026 emphasis on Basel III/G-SIB impacts, Markets balance sheet growth constraints, and AI-related deposit tools and cyber risk. (Analysts; Executive VP & CFO Barnum; Chairman & CEO Dimon)

Risks and concerns

  • On geopolitics, Barnum warned that "developments in the Middle East could have an impact on deal execution and timing" and said reserves could adjust if downside outcomes and "higher energy prices" flow through the economic outlook. (Executive VP & CFO Barnum)

  • On regulatory capital, Barnum said the proposed framework implies JPMorgan’s outcomes are "worse in each category" and Dimon criticized stress frameworks, saying the global market shock is "nothing like what they have" and operational risk RWA "locks up a lot of capital and liquidity for eternity." (Executive VP & CFO Barnum; Chairman & CEO Dimon)

  • On credit cycles and private credit, Dimon said, "there will be a credit cycle 1 day" and "losses will be worse than people expect relative to the scenario," while reiterating, "I don't think it's systemic." (Chairman & CEO Dimon)

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a strong quarter with $16.5 billion of net income and EPS of $5.94, while keeping full-year guideposts intact (NII ex-Markets about $95 billion; total NII about $103 billion; adjusted expenses about $105 billion; card net charge-offs about 3.4%). The most forceful new emphasis was regulatory: management said it is planning for a 5.2% G-SIB requirement in 2028 and described Basel III endgame and G-SIB methodologies as potentially raising costs and constraining Markets balance sheet growth. In Q&A, management repeatedly pointed to resilient consumer trends, no “bad volatility” in trading conditions this quarter, and continued engagement in payments innovation and AI, alongside heightened attention to cyber risk and the possibility that a future credit cycle could be “worse than people expect.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goldman Sachs files Bitcoin ETF targeting steady income, not just gains
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) has reportedly entered the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) ETF space with a filing for a new structured product called the "Goldman Sachs Bitcoin Premium Income ETF," according to a recent X post by Bloomberg ETF analyst Eric Balchunas and regulatory SEC filings shared on Tuesday.

Eric Balchunas recently reacted, saying, "Can't say I saw this coming," adding that he earlier expected banks like JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) to stay out of crypto and focus on other financial products instead.

The idea of this ETF is simple: it gives investors exposure to Bitcoin (BTC-USD) without actually holding the asset directly. 

Instead of directly buying, the fund will invest in Bitcoin-linked products like exchange-traded products (ETPs), options on those products, and indexes that track them. To manage regulatory rules, it may also use a Cayman Islands subsidiary.

According to the filing, at least 80% of the fund’s net assets will be invested in investments that provide Bitcoin (BTC-USD) exposure. This includes spot Bitcoin ETPs, Bitcoin ETP options, and index-linked options tied to Bitcoin ETPs.

Importantly, neither the main fund nor its Cayman Islands subsidiary will directly invest in the asset. However, the Cayman Islands subsidiary may still invest in spot Bitcoin ETPs, along with cash equivalents like U.S. Treasuries, volatility derivatives, and foreign currency forwards. 

The core strategy is an options “overwrite” model, where the fund is expected to sell call options covering roughly 40% to 100% of its Bitcoin-linked exposure. This structure allows the fund to earn option premiums in exchange for limiting upside in strong Bitcoin (BTC-USD) rallies.

Reportedly, the fund is also designed to provide monthly payouts to investors. However, some of these payouts may be treated as “return of capital” for tax purposes.

The filing also lists some major risks, such as BTC's high price volatility, regulatory uncertainty, possible security issues like hacking, and challenges in pricing and liquidity for Bitcoin-related options. There are also structural risks linked to the usage of a Cayman Islands subsidiary.

Meanwhile, BlackRock (BLK) is also working on a similar product called the iShares Bitcoin Premium Income ETF (BITA). This shows a growing trend where big asset managers are now competing to offer Bitcoin products that focus more on steady income rather than just price gains.
